speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Leopold Zylberman. This is tape nine. Leopold, um, what happened in your personal life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after Lola has died, I was very, very depressed. I never went back into her bedroom. No, and then I made a cruise. And then I went to Brazil to my niece's lease wedding. And I came back and I sold the house. And I lived in a flat very modestly. And with the business, I found different articles and switched over to t-shirts. You know, that makes the cost of business, I sold at least my part, I sold to my working partner. And uh, I was going around. And exactly I was meeting people or something. And the meeting go, you see one girl took me to a meeting of singles. And I met Helena. And we sort of took to each other. And say, it took us a year or two until we decided that we, you see, settle in. You know, and I, at that time, you see, we were looking for accommodation. You know, there was-- she had a boy. And my daughter got married six weeks after my wife died, after Lola died. Then they went overseas. And I went to Israel to meet them to go to Ann's first 21st birthday. And I came back. And see, life wasn't settled. I was just off and on. And eventually we sort of came together and decided to go out on a holiday. And on the way back, and then I started looking for accommodation. And one day an agent rang me up and said that this flat is available. So I just put on my pants and went and paid a deposit. Because I've been looking at a lot of flats and took liking to this. But it wasn't-- it was sold to another party who just canceled the contract. And then was the, you see, that means this was good because it has two bathroom. And with a 14, 15 years old boy, you know, it was just like make--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Helena's son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My stepson. It's Mark David White. You see, and he in the top solicitor. You see, and then we planned the wedding, you see, while we had already the accommodation. And Ann was about to have a baby. So I didn't want to be on a honeymoon when the baby is born. So Leigh, my oldest granddaughter, was born on the 11th of March. And exactly on the 11th of April we got married. Because it was sort of like a boy matter, there was a period that you must not marry. But it was the right day, exactly months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: A little take just to finish off, after everything that you experienced, and the losses in your life, and the times you had during that war period, for people who are watching this video, perhaps your grandchildren in the future, do you have anything to say about what occurred to you and any thoughts you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a pretty hard life. But as I told you, I was the youngest, you see, in a family of six. And I got used to being pushed around. [LAUGHS] And that was my destiny, more or less. But anyway, I made a lot of friends. And you see, that missed the question of-- the Jewish question of the Holocaust. When we were liberated for the first time, we looked after ourselves. We just stopped thinking of what happened there and grabbing what came to us. And most, you see, I wasn't in the ghetto. And I didn't see a crematorium or whatever a thing. But you see, it made on me a worse impression than on the people who came out of the camps. Because a lot of people came out of the camps and arrived here. They organized clubs and dances and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And years later, they relax and they started thinking, how did this happen? You know, and well, you have today. In the meantime, I had a few heart attacks. I had a stroke. And I have to take it day after day that it comes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sort of Helena and I, we have the same sense of humor. Not too many fights, you see. And uh, you see, I am the father to Mark because he never seen his father. That means his father died when he was five. But you know, I gave him the direction. I was just-- in a joke I told I told him he would do better if he learned upholstery or something. But you see, his grandfather was a lawyer, both of them, great grandfather. And he studied. He went to study law. I just helped him a little bit financially with pocket money and something so he didn't have-- he could concentrate and work hard. Well, he has two sets of twins. You see, which is pretty hard on him. And he has very little time for his mama. My daughter, who was married, as I told you, six weeks after my wife's death, she had two children. And then after 10 years or so, 12 years, she divorced her husband. And within a year or two, she got herself a second husband. You see, when she started studying pharmacy, and he was criticizing her. A pharmacist is a glorified shopkeeper. And when she met the other fellow, you see a shopkeeper. [LAUGHING] You know, they run a leather business, a few shops in the city and suburbs. And they had a new daughter together. That means he brought in two daughters and she brought in two daughters. And one-- and one they made together. So it's five maidens you see in the household.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you've come out of all this with a large extended family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some think I came out with a family. And I came up with the peace of mind because she can manage. And everybody can manage on its own. And I just hope that I won't suffer too much before I go.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take-- thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each. Simon will give you the cue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's a photo of my daughter, in the middle. Her name is Ann. You see on the left, my oldest granddaughter, whose name is Leigh, L-E-I-G-H. The young blonde in the front is my 10 years youngest granddaughter Amee, A-M-E-E. And on the right is my daughter-- granddaughter Justine, who will be 21 next week.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken some three months ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whereabouts?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a garden, because we went to a wedding. And they all, you see, in a sort of a tent. And before the wedding we took, my son-in-law took the photo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is my stepson Mark. A photo on his graduation, 1979, taken by a photographer at the Monash University.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a photo of Mark's four children, two sets of twins. And left to right, it's Danielle who is eight, and Gerald, eight. And the next one is Bradley. And on the right is Graham. The photo was taken by a photographer, by a professional photographer to their, I think, seventh birthday of the older couple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, now introducing my wife Helena. We married in 1973, which is about 24 years now. And somehow we sort of, uh, live together. And we're very happy. And can look after each other and after the kids. And she is a good stepmother for my daughter and her children, her children. And I'm trying to be good for her son and his children. And now we became a sort of a larger family, which makes me much happier than being on my own. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to express, thanks to Lutik. Thanks to whoever arranged that we are married again in a later stage of life. And we're very lucky to find each other. And we found love and attraction. And Lutik had been a very good father to my son, which he lost his father when he was five years old. And he has been good husband. And I wish him a lot of health. Also, I would like to express thanks to Reuben for his patient and good interview. And that about all I want to say. Thank you.
